text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

One in four trains into Paris is cancelled on the opening day of the Olympics after a co-ordinated attack on the French rail system

When the wiry representative from GB taekwondo hastily left his seat and purposely walked down the carriage, it was a sign that patience on board the Eurostar had worn very thin. His is a sport in which self-control was a key tenet. And who knows what sort of damage he could have done to a hapless conductor.

We were stopped at Lille for no apparent reason, having left late from King’s Cross due to a series ofsabotages on the French high-speed railway network. The train was full – a smattering ofTeam GBrepresentatives (nobody instantly recognisable – they were all safely ensconced in their holding camps), tourists and media.

And after the seventh utterance of “we are very sorry for your inconvenience” in two languages, the feeling of bonhomie and joie de vivre had drained from the train.

When the girl of around six shouted in a North American accent “I am BORED of this”, the rest of the carriage empathised. We were going to be at least an hour late arriving into Paris.

At Lille, the conductor informed us that we would be moving “shortly”. Relative to what remained a mystery. But at least there was a prospect of moving.

One in four trains were cancelled on Friday, which was not a good look for France on the day it is trying to show itself off to the world as open and peaceful.

It wasn’t Eurostar’s fault, of course. This was apparently a co-ordinated attack on signal boxes, designed for maximum impact, but with minimal damage to human life.

Indeed, we could all be thankful that nobody was hurt or killed. The vibe was more “annoyed by industrial action” than “we’re in the middle of an international incident”.

And the train staff were calm, friendly and as informative as they could be. They gained bonus points for handing out little chocolates emblazoned with logos of variousOlympicgoverning bodies. Mine was team Netherlands.

As the train groaned out of Lille and finally began to pick up speed, the GB taekwondo guy came back. Turns out he had only gone out to get a cup of coffee. See? Self-control. His behaviour was an example to all of us.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: What it was like on Eurostar to Paris during Olympic travel chaos
author: Matt Butler
description: One in four trains into Paris is cancelled on the opening day of the Olympics after a co-ordinated attack on the French rail system
